summary: Gruber admits he was wrong about the iPhone 5C being a low-cost play, arguing instead that it's about establishing a two-tier product family akin to MacBook Air/Pro. The 5C is essentially an iPhone 5 in colorful plastic shells at the same price points Apple has always used, making the middle tier more appealing with its own brand identity. He sees the 5S as genuinely forward-thinking with its 64-bit chip, motion coprocessor, upgraded camera, and Touch ID fingerprint sensor. The 5C looks perfect with iOS 7's new design language, while the 5S positions Apple to threaten both the point-and-shoot camera industry and dedicated fitness trackers. Gruber speculates about ARM-based MacBooks and predicts the gold 5S will be wildly popular.
key_points: The iPhone 5C is not about price — it's about creating a two-tier product family like MacBook Air/Pro, with the 5C as the accessible sibling and 5S as the flagship
The 5S's 64-bit ARM chip is 'desktop caliber' performance that raises the question of ARM-based MacBooks in the near future
The M7 motion coprocessor enables always-on fitness tracking with minimal battery impact, potentially threatening dedicated fitness hardware like Fitbit
Touch ID is fast, accurate, and immediately makes passcode entry feel antiquated
Apple is systematically gunning to replace point-and-shoot cameras through software innovation where traditional camera companies can't compete
money_quotes: The iPhone 5C has nothing to do with price.
In marketing, what looks new is new.
The iPhone is growing up as a product family.
This is the first year when last year's specs remain good enough to serve as the mass market new iPhone.
With no hyperbole, I think Apple is gunning to obviate the point-and-shoot camera industry.
I already feel silly tapping in my passcode to unlock my iPhone.
Now that they've gone 64-bit, I've got to start wondering about ARM-based MacBooks in the near future.
key_insight: The iPhone 5C isn't Apple going downmarket on price — it's Apple maturing the iPhone into a two-tier product family where last year's technology, repackaged with fresh design, becomes the mass-market flagship.
